coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from canon ball fruit for sensitive detection of Fe3+ and catalytic reduction of textile dyes
coredata.dc:creator: Ashok Varman, G.
Kalanidhi, K.
Nagaraaj, P.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a simple, sustainable and economically feasible approach has been reported to synthesis Carbon Dots (CDs) by taking Canon Ball (CB) fruit as a bio-mass precursor. The properties of the synthesized CDs were analysed using various characterization techniques such as TEM, FTIR, PSA, Raman, UV–Visible and Fluorescence spectroscopy. The CDs have particle size of 11.2 nm, showed blue emission when exposing to UV light at wavelength of 365 nm and excitation dependent fluorescence behaviour with appreciable quantum yield of 7.01%. The synthesized CDs have excellent stability on exposure to different environments like pH, ionic concentrations and storage time. Further, it served as a probe for sensing Fe3+ ions selectively among different metal ions investigated. The Limit of Detection (LOD) was found to be 0.071 μM in the dynamic range of 0.6–3.3 μM of ferric ion concentration. Additionally, the CDs showed excellent catalytic activity in the treatment of two harmful textile dyes Azure B (AB) and Toluidine Blue (TB) via catalytic reduction with the assistance of NaBH4.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Canon ball fruit
Fe3+ sensing
Catalyst
Azure B
Toluidine B

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology, size and surface functional groups

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a,b)-TEM images (inset: SAED Patten of CDs); (c)- Size distribution (d)-DLS Spectrum; (e)-FT-IR Spectrum; (f)Raman spectrum of the synthesized CDs.
(a)-UV-Visible spectrum; (b)-Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths; (c)-CDs solution in Day light and in UV lamp.
Fluorescence response of CDs in the presence of (a)-different metal ions; (b)- different metal ions individually and with Fe3+ ions.

Fluorescence response of CDs in the presence of (a)-different Concentration of Fe3+ ions; (b)-Relationship curve between the fluorescence intensity variations with the different concentrations of Fe3+; (c)-Linear plot between fluorescence variation and different concentrations of Fe3+ ions.
UV–Visible Spectrum shows the (a)- Reduction of AB with NaBH4 alone; (b)-Catalytic Reduction with NaBH4 in the presence of CDs.
The visual images of AB degradation where, (A) – Azure B; (B) – AB + NaBH4; (C) – AB + NaBH4 + CDs.
The reduction reaction of the AB Dye.
The reduction reaction of TB dye.
UV–Visible Spectrum of TB (a)- Reduction of TB with NaBH4 alone; (b)-Catalytic Reduction of TB with NaBH4 in the presence of CDs.
The visual images of TB degradation where, (A) – Toluidine B; (B) – TB + NaBH4; (C) – TB + NaBH4 + CDs.
Schematic representation of the preparation of carbon dots from CB fruit by HT method.
Schematic representation involves the quenching mechanism of CDs by Fe3+ Ions.
Schematic representation of catalytic activity of CDs towards dyes.
Comparison of different fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ions.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from canon ball fruit for sensitive detection of Fe3+ and catalytic reduction of textile dyes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Anna University, Chennai, 600025, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Anna University, Chennai, 600025, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a simple, sustainable and economically feasible approach has been reported to synthesis Carbon Dots (CDs) by taking Canon Ball (CB) fruit as a bio-mass precursor. The properties of the synthesized CDs were analysed using various characterization techniques such as TEM, FTIR, PSA, Raman, UV–Visible and Fluorescence spectroscopy. The CDs have particle size of 11.2 nm, showed blue emission when exposing to UV light at wavelength of 365 nm and excitation dependent fluorescence behaviour with appreciable quantum yield of 7.01%. The synthesized CDs have excellent stability on exposure to different environments like pH, ionic concentrations and storage time. Further, it served as a probe for sensing Fe3+ ions selectively among different metal ions investigated. The Limit of Detection (LOD) was found to be 0.071 μM in the dynamic range of 0.6–3.3 μM of ferric ion concentration. Additionally, the CDs showed excellent catalytic activity in the treatment of two harmful textile dyes Azure B (AB) and Toluidine Blue (TB) via catalytic reduction with the assistance of NaBH4.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal ions are important to maintain the human body healthy since several biological functions in our body depend upon their presence and several enzymes require those elements for their catalytic reaction [1,2]. Among the all essential metal ions (i.e., sodium, calcium, zinc, magnesium, cobalt, etc.) for normal biological functions of the human body, Iron is the most notable and well known one for its function as a component in proteins such as central metal ion in the blood protein haemoglobin of human blood. It plays crucial role in various enzymatic reactions via electron transfer reactions due to its ability to mediate electron transfer and biological functions such as cellular respiration, energy production, oxygen transport, etc., Though being an indispensable element, presence of excess iron/extended exposure or its deficiency can cause several molecular level diseases and various blood disorders such as haemochromatosis, anaemia, lower immunity, and lower blood pressure [3–7]. Hence, its concentration should be maintained within proper range in order to maintain sustaining function of our body. Therefore, it is important to monitor the iron content in the body to prevent both deficiency and overload in the aspects of human health and disease.

Detection of metal ions at low concentration levels remains always challenging task. Many methods includes spectroscopic and electroanalytical techniques known for the detection of metal ions such as Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS) [8], Inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [9] and electrochemical assays such as Voltammetry [10], etc., which are time consuming and they require expensive instrumentation techniques, cautious sample preparation, etc., For this reason, simple, effective and eco-friendly designing of sensors need to be given much importance for the detection of metal ions [11]. Among the different methods for that purpose, fluorescence probes as sensors have gained much attention due to numerous advantages such as high quantum yield, ease of modification, good biocompatibility, high sensitivity, and mainly in real-time sensing applications [12–14].

Water pollution is another major environmental crisis which directly impacts the worst results on human health as water is being primarily used for basic and developmental processes for humans and it is essential to us and other living things for drinking. Discharging of industrial wastes as effluent (such as Textile, leather, Tanning industries), household sewage and drainage dumping, radioactive wastes are the primary reasons for water pollution. Also, fertilizers and pesticides used in agricultural fields can affect the quality of the water bodies. Those industrial wastes and heavy metals disposed from them can accumulate in water bodies which in turn makes our community to face serious threats to our life and as well as animals [15,16].

Among all other sources, textile industries are the major contributors to water pollution. Azure B (AB) and Toluidine Blue (TB) are widely used in textile and other industries belonging to the thiazine (Quinone¬imine) group of dyes. Though the dyes are used in several fields for various applications, they possess serious impacts on health. AB has potential mutagenic activity in living things. It exhibits inherent toxicity through intercalation into DNA, by partitioning into the membrane lipid of biological cells. Also, AB exhibits cytotoxicity by acting as a reversible inhibitor of enzymes which plays important role in human behaviour. Similarly, TB is an animal mutagen and a skin irritant. It possess high toxicity to humans include anemia, anorexia, weight loss, skin lesions, central nervous system depression, cyanosis, and methemoglobinemia [17–22].

Because of the toxic nature of these dyes, it is necessary to concern about removal of these dyes from the effluents. Many research works such as photocatalytic and bio-degradation have been reported for the degradation of these dyes. For example, Haq I. and his group explored the biodegradation of AB dye by Serratia liquefaciens with 90% decolourization observed in 48 h [19]. In another work, Li. H and his co-workers have isolated Bacillus sp. MZS10 bacterium for the treatment of AB dye with 93% decolourization in 14 h [20]. T. Arthi et al., used solution combustion synthesized TiO2 as photocatalyst to degrade Azure A and Azure B dyes [21]. N. Neelakandeswari et al. prepared Cadmium Sulphide nanoparticles and used as a photocatalyst for the degradation of TB dye with 90% maximum degradation in 170 min [22]. However, these methods are limited to apply for large scale implementations. Hence, we reported a simple, effective and less time consuming approach by using Carbon Dots (CDs) as a catalyst for the reduction of AB and TB dyes with the assistance of NaBH4 as a reducing agent.

CDs are promising fluorescent nanomaterials belonging to the carbon family and are widely used in various sectors such as sensor, catalysis, bio imaging etc., due to its outstanding properties. They became very popular in a short period over the past decade for their low cost preparation, green nature in terms of low toxicity, biocompatibility, distinct and tunable optical, physical and chemical properties, in addition to that availability of wide range of precursors (including chemical and natural) for their synthesis [23–29]. Herein, we have proposed to utilize CDs as a sensing probe as well as catalyst due to its excellent fluorescence property and catalytic activity.

Since its discovery, various new, novel and rapid strategies have been explored for the synthesis and modification of CDs by different types of physical and chemical methods such as hydrothermal (HT) treatment [30–34], electrochemical method [35,36], laser ablation [37] and microwave treatment [38,39], neutralisation reactions [40], etc., However, HT synthesis of CDs remains always considerable in most cases since it provides simple and controlled conditions for reactions to take place. One of the most advantages of the HT method reported is that it can favor self-passivation of CDs without any post-treatment [41].

Many research works have been successfully presented for the preparation of CDs by using a range of chemical precursors with different synthetic protocols. For example CDs from ammonium citrate [30], urea and p-phenylenediammine [31], m-phenylenediammine [32], polyvinylpyrrolidine [42], glycine [43], etc., However, these precursors can limit their usage by means of toxicity, extreme reaction conditions and post-treatments. Hence, being a researcher we must ensure sustainability by avoiding those difficulties mentioned above throughout the synthesis steps. For the attainment of the above reasons, a green synthesis strategy can be helpful. Among various methods in green synthesis, usage of bio-mass precursors is highly attractive sight of research since its usage will reduce the use of toxic chemicals, improve the bio-refinery profits and importantly, it will respect the environment [44–48].

In many research works, CDs as fluorimetric sensor with range of limit of detection (LOD) values derived from variety of precursors were reported for the detection of Fe3+ ions. For example, Li and his co-workers synthesized N-doped CDs by hydrothermally from dopamine and ethylenediamine to detect Fe3+ ions with LOD of 10.8 μM [33]. Z.M.S.H. Khan et al. reported that water soluble N-CQDs prepared by HT treatment of red lentils were used for the detection of Fe3+ with LOD 0.10 μM [34]. J. Shen and his co-workers employed sweet potatoes as bio-precursors to synthesize CDs with LOD around 0.32 μM [41]. N. Wang et al. proposed one-step hydrothermal method to prepare CDs from papaya powder as bio-source with LOD of 0.48 μM [49]. J. Ahn et al. synthesized FWCDs from sandwich leftover food waste by one-step HT carbonization method with LOD of 32 μM to Fe3+ ions [50]. Keeping the requirements which mentioned above in mind and comparing the other works, we approached Couroupita guianensis fruit as bio-mass source for this work to prepare CDs by one-step HT synthesis method with LOD as much as lower on comparison with other works.

Couroupita guianensis commonly called as a Canon Ball (CB) tree, is a tropical tree native to South America and spread all over the world. It is a flowering tree that belongs to the family lecythidacea, and also a medicinal plant that constitutes compounds like couroupitine-A, couroupitine-B, indurubin, terpenoids, ketosteroids, glycosides, isatin, and phenolic substances etc. [51,52]. Though being a medicinal plant [53], major portions of this plant are being unused and getting wasted and hence it can be considered as a useful bio-mass. Also, HT treatment of this fruit may lead to the polymerization and carbonization of the compounds available in it into CDs because of high pressure relatively at low temperature.

In this work, we have used a green approach for synthesis of CDs from CB fruit by a one-pot HT route. The synthesized CDs are thoroughly characterized by TEM, FTIR, PSA, Raman, UV–Visible, and Fluorescence spectroscopy. It shows excellent bright blue emission under UV lamp, good storage stability, ionic stability, pH dependent and water solubility. It exhibits excellent selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+ ions with LOD of 0.071 μM. Later, the catalytic activity of synthesized CDs was studied towards AB and TB dyes.

The CB fruit was collected from local Government School at Kanchipuram, Tamilnadu. Chemicals such as Quinine Sulfate, Inorganic salts – CdCl2.H2O, HgSO4, Co(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, KI, ZnSO4, FeCl3, Na2SO4, MnCl2.4H2O, AgNO3 were purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories (SRL) Private Limited, India. Sulphuric Acid was purchased from Merck, India. Since all the chemicals were in analytical grade, they were used without any further purification.

The morphology and particle size were examined through Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) analysis (JEOL, Japan). The average size of the CDs was analysed by using HORIBA SZ-100 Diffuse Light Scattering Instrument at scattering angle of 90° at temperature 24.9 °C. UV–Visible spectra of CDs were recorded in the range of 200–800 nm using JASCO V-770 Spectrophotometer. FT-IR spectrum of CDs was recorded in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 by ATR method using BRUKAR FT-IR spectrometer. Raman Spectrum of the CDs was recorded by using HORIBA Labspec 6 Confocal Raman Microscope with a laser excitation wavelength of 532 nm. All the Fluorescence studies were carried out by recording the fluorescence spectrum using a spectrophotometer (Ocean Optics, USD 2000+) at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The fluorescence life-time measurement was taken from HORIBA, FLUOROLOG 3 TCSPC.

The schematic representation of the CDs preparation from CB fruit was shown in Scheme 1 . Briefly, the pulp of the CB fruit was smashed and the juice was filtered to remove large pulps. 20 mL of the juice was taken and 30 mL of distilled water was added to it and transferred into a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and then heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After being allowed for cooling naturally to room temperature, the obtained brown-yellow solution was centrifuged and filtered using membrane filter paper. The filtered solution was stored for further studies [54,55].

Quantum Yield (QY) of the synthesized CDs was calculated by using Quinine Sulfate (QS) as standard reference (QY 54%). The reference solution was prepared by dissolving QS in 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution and the QY of the CDs were calculated using the following formula (1).
(1)
φ cd
=
φ qs
×
І cd
І qs
×
A cd
A qs
×
γ cd 2
γ qs 2
Where φ is the QY of the CDs, I is the integrated emission intensity, A is absorbance at λ = 380 nm and γ is the refractive index and the subscripts ‘cd and qs’ refers CDs and reference respectively [34,43,49,56–58].

The stability of the CDs was investigated by recording the PL spectrum of the synthesized CDs with solutions of different environments like pH (2, 4, 6, 7, 9, 10, 12, 14), ionic concentration {concentration of NaCl [(M) = 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 and 1]}, and storage time in 1:1 ratio [34,49,58].

All experiments were performed at neutral pH in an aqueous medium using distilled water. Briefly, 100 μL of CDs solution was mixed with 1 mL of distilled water and followed by 100 μL volume (1:1 ratio) of different metal ions (333 μM) individually. Then the final volume was made up to 3 mL and the corresponding PL spectra were recorded. Further sensitivity, selectivity, and LOD were investigated by adding different concentration of Fe3+ ions.

The catalytic properties of the synthesized CDs were studied via reducing two textiles dyes, (AB, TB) by recording the change in absorbance at their corresponding maximum wavelength (λmax ) in UV–Vis spectrum, using CDs as catalyst and 0.1 M NaBH4solution as reducing agent. In a typical procedure, 3 mL volume of 10 ppm concentration of the dyes solutions were taken in a test tube and different volumes of NaBH4 were added and corresponding absorbance on UV–Visible spectrum was recorded. The same procedure was adopted on adding different volumes of CDs along with NaBH4 and the reduction reaction was monitored by recording the corresponding absorbance on UV–Visible Spectrum. Various optimization studies like dye concentration, reaction time, etc., were carried out to study their effects on reduction reaction and thus from those experiments, optimum concentration of catalyst and the reducing agent were found [59,60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, size and surface functional groups
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the morphology and size of the synthesized CDs, TEM analysis has been performed. Fig. 1 -(a–c) displays the surface morphology and histogram of the synthesized CDs. It exhibits homogenous spherical morphology with a size of less than 15 nm. This result is also supported by DLS analysis which was taken by diluting the as prepared CDs with distilled water in the ratio of 1:300. From the results shown in Fig. 1-(d), it was found that CDs have an average particle size of 11.2 nm. The SAED pattern (inset Fig. 1-(b)) shows diffused rings which explain the amorphous nature of CDs. The functional groups present on the surface of the CDs were explored with the help of the FT-IR spectroscopy taken by ATR method in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. The recorded spectrum of the CDs was shown in Fig. 1-(e). The broad and strong absorption band at 3331 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H functional groups [30–34,49]. A strong peak observed at 1634 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of CO groups which is present on the surface of the prepared CDs [30,32,34,36,58]. Thus, from the FT-IR spectra of the synthesized CDs, it is clearly known that oxygen rich groups are present on the surface of the CDs, which gives them the ultimate solubility and dispensability in aqueous medium. Raman spectroscopy is a powerful tool to characterize the carbon based nanomaterials. Fig. 1-(f) shows the raman spectrum of CDs which exhibits two raman bands at 1331 cm−1 and 1563 cm−1 are attributed to D band and G band. D band is due to the presence of surface defects whereas G band is related to Sp2 hybridized hexagonal carbon atom. The ratio of ID/IG indicates the well graphitized carbon structure which found to be 0.85. The results clearly explain the existence of a defective carbon structure that has been formed in the synthesized CDs [31,36,61,62].

Fig. 2 -(a) shows the UV–Visible spectrum of the synthesized CDs in which a strong absorption peak observed at 280 nm in UV region belongs to the π-π* (CC) transitions of aromatic carbon structure of CDs [31,36,39,43]. Fig. 2-(b) shows the fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 to 480 nm. From the emission spectrum, it is observed that CDs shows a strong emission peak with maximum intensity at 380 nm wavelength. Because of the excitation dependent emission characteristics of CDs (agreeable with many literatures) [34,41,61–63] on increasing the wavelength from 320 to 480 nm, the intensity of the emission peak increases, shifts towards a longer wavelength and then decreases. This is due to the presence of different surface functional groups and the formation of different particle sizes of CDs. Fig. 2-(c) shows that CDs solution is in yellow color in normal daylight whereas on exposing it under UV lamp at wavelength 365 nm which exhibit blue fluorescence. Further to study the fluorescence properties, QY of the as prepared CDs were calculated taking QS (54%) as standard reference (Fig. S1). The QY is found to be 7.24%.

To study the stability of the CDs in different environments, they were exposed to various conditions like different pH, different ionic concentrations and different storage times. The fluorescence spectrum of CDs was performed with different pH values ranging from 2 to 12 and in difference ionic concentrations of NaCl and at different regular intervals of storage time of 30 days. Fig. S2 shows a plot between the intensity of emission peak Vs (a)-different pH values; (b)-different concentrations of NaCl and (c)-different storage time of the CDs in which F is the emission intensity of the CDs on adding different pH solutions whereas F0 is the emission intensity of the CDs alone. From that Figs. S2–(a), it is observed that though there is a slight decrease in emission intensity in low pH (i.e. acidic condition) but no significant change is observed in higher pH. Hence, further studies were carried out in neutral pH throughout the experiment. The influence of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of the prepared CDs was investigated by adding different concentrations of NaCl solution. Figs. S2–(b) shows the influence of various concentrations of NaCl ranging from 0.2 to 1 M on CDs emission intensity. The results show that no changes in the intensity were observed during the addition of NaCl solution. Hence, we can conclude that various ionic concentrations do not influence the emission intensity. To investigate the stability of the CDs for a long storage time, they were stored at room temperature for 30 days and the fluorescence spectra were recorded for every five days once. From these results (Figs. S2–(c))it was confirmed that CDs are very stable for long periods and also there is no suspensions or precipitated particles were found during the storage period [34,49,58].

The fluorescence intensity of CDs was monitored by adding different metal ions like Cd2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, K+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Na+, Mn2+, Ag+ in various conditions. Fig. 3 -(a) shows the fluorescence response of CDs in the presence of different metal ions. From Fig. 3 -(a), it is clearly known that fluorescence intensity decreases to the maximum when Fe3+ions was added to the CDs solution. Whereas other metal ions of the same concentration when added, there are no maximum decrease in intensity observed. Hence, we can conclude that the as prepared CDs sensor is specific for Fe3+ ions.

Further to study the competitive analysis of CDs sensor to Fe3+ ions, various metal ions were incorporated along with Fe3+and the corresponding fluorescence responses were recorded. Fig. 3-(b) shows, the fluorescence response of CDs with different metal ions individually and along with Fe3+ ions. It clearly observed that Fe3+ ions only decrease the fluorescence intensity of the CDs among all the other metal ions. Hence, we can confirm that synthesized CDs have specific selectivity towards Fe3+ ions.

To investigate the extent of CDs sensor for the detection of Fe3+ ions, different concentrations of Fe3+ solutions were added to the CDs solution and the fluorescence response and absorbance measurements were recorded. Fig. 4-(a) shows the fluorescence response of CDs with different concentration of Fe3+ ions. It exhibits a decrease in emission intensity with increasing concentration of Fe3+ as it displays a good sensitivity towards Fe3+ ions. Whereas, the absorbance spectrum of CDs increases with increasing concentration of Fe3+and prominence of the peak at 280 nm decreases, indicating a strong interaction between the CDs and Fe3+(Fig. S4). The response graph was plotted between relative fluorescence emission intensity Vs concentration of Fe3+ ions depicted in Fig. 4-(b).

The plot explains that the fluorescence quenching is linear in the concentration range 0.6–3.3 μM. Hence linear plot was plotted [Fig. 4-(c)] to that range with correlation factor (R2) = 0.9977 and linear equation is as follows (Eq. (2))
(2) F0/F – 1 = 0.04889 (Fe3+) + 0.05321
where Fo is the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence of Fe3+ and F is the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence of Fe3+. The LOD was obtained using the formula LOD = 3σ/K, where σ is the standard deviation and K is the slope of the line [34,41,43,58]. Thus, the LOD was found to be 0.071 μM which is better than previously reported literatures for CDs without any doping (The reported results were given in Table 1 .

The possible quenching mechanism of the CDs by Fe3+ ions is shown in Scheme 2 and to confirm the actual mechanism, further it was studied with the help of life-time analysis by recording time dependent fluorescence life-time spectra at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Fig. S3 shows life-time decay curve of the CDs (a) in the absence and (b) in the presence of Fe3+ ions and it had been best fitted with tri-exponential fit given by the expression (3)
(3) I(t) = α1exp (t/τ1) + α2exp(t/τ2) + α3exp(t/τ3)
Where τ1,τ2,τ3 are different decay times and α1,α2,α3 are corresponding amplitudes [34]. The values are given in Table 2 . From the values obtained from the decay curve, the average life-time of CDs was calculated using the following formula (4).
(4)
Average Life Time =
Σ
τ i 2
α i 2
Σ
τ i
α i

The average life-time of CDs in the absence of Fe3+ ions was found to be 0.1075 ns whereas in the presence of Fe3+ ions was 0.0979 ns. [34,58]. Since, there was no significant change observed in an average life-time (difference between the τ-averages is 0.0096 ns) on adding Fe3+ ions, it confirms that fluorescence quenching was caused by static quenching mechanism which involves the formation of the complex between the donor fluorophore and acceptor chromophore in the ground state by ignoring the excited state interactions [12,34,41,49,57,58]. Here, the donor fluorophore is CDs and the acceptor chromophore is Fe3+ ions. The complexation may be due to the presence of oxygen rich surface moieties like hydroxyl and carboxylic groups, which easily can interact with the Fe3+ ions.
The catalytic activity of CDs was investigated by the catalytic reduction of two textile dyes AB and TB with the assistance of NaBH4.

The reduction reaction was monitored with UV–Visible spectroscopy by recording the decrease in the absorbance peak of the dyes at different time intervals. Adding various concentrations of NaBH4 alone does not reduce the dyes to the appreciable percentage (maximum) even though the reaction was allowed for long time. But when a minimum amount of CDs is added along with NaBH4, it is observed that the dye gets reduced to the maximum within less than 6 min. This reveals that synthesized CDs acts as catalyst for the reaction which facilitates the transfer of hydrogen and electrons to the dye molecules. As shown in Scheme 3 , the dye molecules and BH4 − ions from the reducing agent get adsorbed on the surface of the CDs, electron transfer takes place and the dye molecules get reduced. The results of the experiments suggest that the synthesized CDs act as an electron transfer medium that vanishes the energy barrier between the reactant and product by enhancing the reaction speed [59,60,64].

From the UV–Vis spectra the percentage of reduction of the dye was calculated using the following formula (5).
(5)
Efficiency =
A 0
− A
A 0
× 100
Where, A0 is the absorbance of dyes and A is the absorbance of the dyes in the presence of reducing agent and CDs.

The synthesized CDs have been used as a catalyst for the reduction of AB using NaBH4. Fig. 5 shows the UV spectrum of the characteristic peak of AB dye at 650 nm on reduction. On adding NaBH4 alone up to 600 μL, the dye gets reduced to 31.75% only even though the reaction was allowed to carry for long time which could be seen from Fig. 5-(a) with decreases in the absorbance corresponding to different volumes of NaBH4 and after that no decrease in absorbance was observed. But, when CDs were added along with NaBH4 in the 1:1 ratio (300 μL), maximum reduction of 93.03% was observed within 6 min shown in Fig. 5-(b)and the rate constant was found to be k = 0.00672 S−1from the kinetics plot (Fig. S5). Fig. 6 displays the visual image of reduction of AB by CDs and the reduction reaction of AB by CDs in the presence of NaBH4 was shown in Fig. 7 .

The same procedure was also followed for the catalytic reduction of TB. The reduction reaction of TB by CDs in the presence of NaBH4 was shown in Fig. 8 . Fig. 9 shows the UV spectrum of the characteristic peak of TB dye at 630 nm on reduction. On adding NaBH4 alone up to 200 μL, the dye gets reduced to 40.01% only even though the reaction was allowed to carry for a long time which could be seen from Fig. 9-(a) with decreases in the absorbance corresponding to different volumes of NaBH4 and after that no decrease in absorbance was observed. But, when CDs added along with NaBH4 in the 1:1 ratio (100 μL), maximum reduction of 94.56% was observed within 3 min shown in Fig. 9-(b) and a kinetics plot was obtained for the reduction of TB dye (Fig. S6) and rate constant was found to be k = 0.01902 S-1. The visual image of the reduction of TB by CDs was shown in Fig. 10 .
The degradation time for catalytic reduction of dyes using synthesized CDs is comparatively low when compared with other reported CDs for catalytic reduction of other dyes [65–67].

In this work, CDs were synthesized from canon ball fruit by a simple hydrothermal method without any chemical modification. It emits bright blue fluorescence under UV lamp and possesses excellent water solubility. It has strong stability on pH, ionic concentration and storage time. The synthesized nanoprobe has effectively been proposed for Fe3+ detection with excellent selectivity and sensitivity and LOD was found to be 0.071 μM. Later, the catalytic activity of synthesized CDs was studied and reveals excellent catalytic activity towards AB and TB dyes with reduction of 93.03% and 94.54% respectively. A sustainable, cost-effective and large scale synthesis of biomass derived CDs could be an efficient nanoprobe for Fe3+ detection and dyes reduction in the environmental samples.

G. Ashok Varman: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. K. Kalanidhi: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. P. Nagaraaj: Technical correction, Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Department of Chemistry, Anna University for constant support and encouragement.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110101.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Roles of metals in human health

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gupta SP. Roles of metals in human health, MOJ Biorg Org Chem. 2018;2(5):221-224.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of metal ions in biological systems: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xian Zheng, Wenyu Cheng, Chendong Ji, Jin Zhang, and Meizhen Yin, Detection of metal ions in biological systems: A review, Reviews in Analytical Chemistry 2020; 39: 231-246.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The molecular biology of human iron metabolism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: William E. Winter, Lindsay A. L. Bazydlo, and Neil S. Harris, The Molecular Biology of Human Iron Metabolism, Lab Med Spring 2014;45:92-102.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Iron(iii) selective molecular and supramolecular fluorescent probes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sahoo, S. K., Sharma, D., Bera, R. K., Crisponi, G., & Callan, J. F. Iron(iii) selective molecular and supramolecular fluorescent probes. Chemical Society Reviews 2020 41(21), 7195.
Brissot P, Pietrangelo A, Adams PC, De Graaff B, McLaren CE, Loreal O. Haemochromatosis. Nature Reviews Disease Primers. 2018;4(1):1-5..

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Iron deficiency anemia: a common and curable disease
